analysis: The broad concept 'sweet_vs_bitter' conflates several distinct semantic dimensions. It doesn't account for the *intensity* of sweetness, the *texture* experienced in the mouth, the *complexity* of the flavor profile, or the *duration* of the aftertaste.  The transformer is likely learning to associate certain words with 'sweet' or 'bitter' without understanding the underlying nuances of these sensory experiences. Decomposing this space into the sub-concepts above allows for a more granular and accurate representation of the taste perception, enabling more precise steered generation and improved understanding of the underlying semantics.

sub_concepts.description: This sub-concept captures the degree of sweetness. High sweetness implies a prominent sugary flavor, while low sweetness or bitterness indicates a lack of sweetness or a presence of bitter notes.
sub_concepts.why_distinct: Distinguishes between subtle sweetness and a strong, overt sugary taste.  It's not simply about 'sweet' vs. 'not sweet', but the *level* of sweetness.
pos_examples: Das Kind: Vanillecreme — mit einer warmen Spur;
L’étudiante : du miel — avec des notes de caramel ;
La barista : du miel — avec une finale douce ;
neg_examples: La barista: radicchio crudo — con un finale lungo e austero;
The violinist held it in the mouth over-extracted coffee,

sub_concepts.description: This sub-concept focuses on the tactile sensation in the mouth. Creamy/smooth refers to a rich, coating texture, while dry/astringent describes a sensation of puckering or roughness.
sub_concepts.why_distinct: Separates the gustatory experience from the physical feeling.  A sweet taste can be creamy, but it can also be dry or astringent.
pos_examples: nahm einen Schluck Vanillecreme: und die Wangen entspannten, mit weichem Abgang;
La cuoca mise una goccia sulla lingua frutta matura,
El pastelero: fruta madura — con notas de caramelo;
neg_examples: masticò un attimo un infuso forte: e arrivò un morso netto,
La barista: radicchio crudo — con un finale lungo e austero;
The sommelier swallowed slowly a strong infusion, and the flavor stayed dry;

sub_concepts.description: This sub-concept distinguishes between flavors with multiple, evolving notes (nuanced/layered) and those that are straightforward and uncomplicated (simple/direct).
sub_concepts.why_distinct: Accounts for the richness and depth of the flavor profile. Some sweet flavors are simple, while others have multiple components.
pos_examples: L’étudiante : du miel — avec des notes de caramel ;
La cuoca mise una goccia sulla lingua frutta matura,
El pastelero: fruta madura — con notas de caramelo;
neg_examples: The child: a strong infusion — with a medicinal note;
La barista tragó despacio chocolate negro intenso,

sub_concepts.description: This sub-concept refers to how long the flavor remains in the mouth after swallowing. A lingering/persistent aftertaste lasts for a noticeable time, while a fleeting/transient one disappears quickly.
sub_concepts.why_distinct: Addresses the temporal aspect of the taste experience.  A sweet flavor can have a long or short aftertaste.
pos_examples: La barista : du miel — avec une finale douce ;
Die Studentin: Honig — mit einer warmen Spur;
neg_examples: The violinist held it in the mouth over-extracted coffee,
La cuoca: caffè troppo estratto — con una nota medicinale;
The barista tragó despacio chocolate negro intenso,
